summary: The proposal treats world modeling as an analytic optimization problem, whereas the underlying dynamics are likely computationally irreducible and require direct simulation.
body: It is now nearly forty years since I began the project that became A New Kind of Science. One of the central observations is that simple programs can generate behavior that is impossible to predict analytically. This paper attempts to build a world model for robotic control, framing the environment as something to be predicted. But I wonder: have you simply run the rules? When I looked at cellular automata in 1983, I found that even the simplest rules could produce complexity that no closed-form equation could capture. If the world model relies on a neural network to approximate the dynamics, it may be fighting against computational irreducibility. The suggestion is to explicitly search the space of simple programs that could reproduce the control dynamics, rather than assuming a gradient-based predictor is sufficient. In the computational universe, the right move is almost always to just run it.
